nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reefs and clear coastal waters with dense coral cover, where the species tends to stay secretive among coral branches on reef crests and slopes.

decorPreferences: Plenty of live rock with caves and crevices for hiding and foraging

diet.notes: Generally accepts a wide range of meaty foods including mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and vitamin-enriched frozen preparations. Tends to actively hunt small crustaceans, flatworms, bristle worms, and pyramidellid snails on live rock, and is often used as a natural pest-control fish for these. Best fed twice daily.

goodWith: Hardy reef-safe species of similar or larger size

summary: The Six Line Wrasse is a small, vividly striped reef fish widely considered hardy and easy to feed, but is commonly reported to become aggressive toward smaller, more peaceful tankmates as it matures.
careNotes: The Six Line Wrasse is generally considered a beginner-friendly reef fish: hardy, colourful, and quick to accept prepared foods. It has been widely reported to actively hunt flatworms, bristle worms, and pyramidellid snails, and is often added to reef tanks as a natural pest-control species. Despite its small adult size (roughly 8–10 cm), it tends to become increasingly territorial and is commonly reported to harass smaller, more peaceful tankmates such as firefish, small gobies, dartfish, mandarins, and even some clownfish — for this reason it is generally advised to add it last to the display and to provide plenty of rockwork so weaker tankmates can escape line-of-sight. Conspecific aggression is high; only one Six Line Wrasse should generally be kept per tank unless the system is very large. The species is a known jumper, so a tightly-fitting lid is recommended. Native to a wide Indo-Pacific range from the Red Sea east to the Tuamotu Islands and north to the Ryukyus, typically on coral-rich reef slopes between roughly 1 and 35 m depth. Like other wrasses, it is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite.
breedingNotes: The Six Line Wrasse is a pelagic spawner in the wild, where pairs broadcast eggs into the water column. Captive breeding has not been commonly reported in the aquarium hobby.

fish.breedingNotes: The Six Line Wrasse spawns in the open water column in the wild, but captive breeding has not been commonly reported in the aquarium hobby.
